summary: DHH shares his enthusiasm for non-linear filmmaking after watching Memento and The Limey. He describes how The Limey's technique of mixing past, present, and future within scenes creates a fascinating viewing experience comparable to Memento's reverse chronology. He wonders why more directors don't experiment with temporal structure and asks readers to recommend similar films.
key_points: Memento opened his eyes to the power of non-linear storytelling in film
The Limey impressed him with its technique of layering past, present, and future within individual scenes, despite a less original plot
He sees non-linear structure as an underused technique that enriches character development
money_quotes: Memento showed me how fascinating a film can be when it doesn't follow a linear trail.
The way most of the scenes are contructed using past, present and future at once is just as fascinating an experience as seing a movie backwards.
It makes the character development a very different, but welcome, experience, and I wonder why more directors haven't used this technique.
key_insight: Non-linear narrative structure in film can transform even a conventional plot into a fascinating experience by reframing how audiences engage with character development.
